flashcards.front: What is thread scheduling in operating systems?
flashcards.back: Thread scheduling is the process of allocating CPU time to threads, deciding which thread runs on the CPU at any given time. It manages the execution order of multiple threads to maximize CPU utilization and system performance.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between preemptive and non-preemptive thread scheduling?
flashcards.back: Preemptive scheduling allows the OS to forcibly remove a thread from CPU even before it completes its time quantum. Non-preemptive scheduling lets a thread run to completion or until it voluntarily yields the CPU.

flashcards.front: What are the five major scheduling criteria?
flashcards.back: 1) CPU Utilization - percentage of time CPU is busy, 2) Throughput - number of threads completed per unit time, 3) Turnaround Time - time from thread arrival to completion, 4) Waiting Time - time thread spends ready in queue, 5) Response Time - time from request to first response.

flashcards.front: What are the five states in the thread lifecycle?
flashcards.back: 1) NEW - thread is being created, 2) READY - thread waiting in queue for CPU, 3) RUNNING - thread executing on CPU, 4) BLOCKED/WAITING - thread waiting for I/O or event, 5) TERMINATED - thread finished execution.

flashcards.front: What is context switching in thread scheduling?
flashcards.back: Context switching is the process of saving the state (registers, program counter, stack pointer) of a currently running thread and loading the state of the next thread to be executed. It introduces overhead.

flashcards.front: What is a thread priority in scheduling?
flashcards.back: Thread priority indicates the importance of a thread relative to others. Higher priority threads get CPU time first. Scheduling can be priority-based (higher priority runs first) or equal priority uses round-robin.

flashcards.front: What is the many-to-one threading model?
flashcards.back: Many-to-one model maps multiple user-level threads to a single kernel thread. All thread management is done in user space. Problem: if one thread blocks on I/O, entire process blocks.

flashcards.front: What is the one-to-one threading model?
flashcards.back: One-to-one model maps each user-level thread to a separate kernel thread. Provides better concurrency than many-to-one but limited by number of kernel threads the OS can support.

flashcards.front: What is the many-to-many threading model?
flashcards.back: Many-to-many model multiplexes many user-level threads to equal or fewer kernel threads. Users can create any number of threads and kernel threads can run in parallel on multi-core systems.

flashcards.front: What is a thread pool and why is it used?
flashcards.back: A thread pool is a collection of pre-created worker threads waiting to execute tasks. It avoids overhead of creating new threads for each task, limits concurrent threads, and improves response time for server applications.

Round-Robin assigns a fixed time quantum to each thread in the ready queue. Threads are executed in circular order. If quantum expires, thread moves to end of queue. Fair but may have high context switching overhead.

First-Come-First-Served (FCFS) schedules threads in order of their arrival. The first thread runs to completion before next starts. Simple but can cause convoy effect where short threads wait for long ones.

flashcards.front: What is SJF (Shortest Job First) scheduling?
flashcards.back: SJF selects the thread with shortest burst time next. It minimizes average waiting time but requires knowing or estimating burst times. Can cause starvation for long threads.

Multilevel queue scheduling maintains separate queues for different thread types (system, interactive, batch). Each queue has its own scheduling algorithm. Queues have priorities with possible starvation prevention.

flashcards.front: What is thread affinity in scheduling?
flashcards.back: Thread affinity binds a thread to specific CPU cores. Hard affinity prevents migration between cores; soft affinity allows migration. Used to improve cache performance by keeping thread on same core.
